# Overview: statistical inference

## The population model (greek letters, no hats) {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   This is an *idealized* representation of the data: $$
    y = \beta_0+\beta_1x +\varepsilon;
    $$

-   There is some "true line" floating out there with a "true slope" $\beta_1$ and "true intercept" $\beta_0$;

-   With infinite amounts of perfectly measured data, we could know $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ exactly;

-   We don't have that, so we must use finite amounts of imperfect data to *estimate*;

-   We are especially interested in $\beta_1$ because is characterizes the association between $x$ and $y$, which is useful for *prediction*.

-   This idea continues to multiple linear regression (additive/interaction) and logistic regression
:::

## "Three branches of statistical government" {.smaller}

We have an unknown quantity we are trying to learn about (for example, $\beta_1$) using noisy, imperfect data.
Learning comes in three flavors:

::: incremental
-   **POINT ESTIMATION**: get a single-number best guess for $\beta_1$;

-   **INTERVAL ESTIMATION**: get a range of likely values for $\beta_1$ that characterizes (sampling) uncertainty;

-   **HYPOTHESIS TESTING**: use the data to distinguish competing claims about $\beta_1$.
:::

. . .

::: callout-important
While the examples we do here follow simple linear regression, the same ideas apply to the other models we have learned so far!!
:::

## Point estimation (roman letters, hats!) {.smaller}

We estimate $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ with the coefficients of the best fit line:

$$
\hat{y}=b_0+b_1x.
$$

-   Linear regression: "Best" means "least squares." We pick the estimates so that the sum of squared residuals is as small as possible.

## Point estimation {.smaller}


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) + 
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](22-more-inference_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-2-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Point estimation {.smaller}


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
linear_reg() |>
  fit(mpg ~ wt, data = mtcars) |>
  tidy()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
# A tibble: 2 × 5
  term        estimate std.error statistic  p.value
  <chr>          <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>
1 (Intercept)    37.3      1.88      19.9  8.24e-19
2 wt             -5.34     0.559     -9.56 1.29e-10
```


:::
:::


## Sampling uncertainty {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   How do our point estimates vary across alternative, hypothetical datasets?

    -   If they vary alot, uncertainty is high, and results are unreliable;
    -   if they vary only a little, uncertainty is low, and results are more reliable.

-   We can use the *bootstrap* to construct alternative datasets and assess the sensitivity of our estimates to changes in the data.
:::

## Bootstrap distribution {.smaller}

This histogram displays variation in the slope estimate across alternative datasets.

```         
- Large spread >> high uncertainty;
- Small spread >> lower uncertainty.
```


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](22-more-inference_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-4-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Confidence interval {.smaller}

Pick a range that swallows up a large % of the histogram:


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](22-more-inference_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


We use quantiles (think IQR) but there are other ways.

## Hypothesis testing {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   Two competing claims about $\beta_1$: $$
    \begin{aligned}
    H_0&: \beta_1=0\quad(\text{nothing going on})\\
    H_A&: \beta_1\neq0\quad(\text{something going on})
    \end{aligned}
    $$

-   Do the data strongly favor one or the other?

-   How can we quantify this?
:::

## Hypothesis testing {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   Think hypothetically: if the null hypothesis were in fact true, would my results be out of the ordinary?

    -   if no, then the null could be true;
    -   if yes, then the null might be bogus;

-   My results represent the reality of actual data.
    If they conflict with the null, then you throw out the null and stick with reality;

-   How do we *quantify* "would my results be out of the ordinary"?
:::

## Null distribution {.smaller}

If the null happened to be true, how would we expect our results to vary across datasets?
We can use simulation to answer this:


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](22-more-inference_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-6-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


This is how the world should look *if the null is true*.

## Null distribution versus reality {.smaller}

Locate the *actual* results of your *actual* data analysis under the null distribution.
Are they in the middle?
Are they in the tails?


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](22-more-inference_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-7-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


Are these results in harmony or conflict with the null?

## Null distribution versus reality {.smaller}

Locate the *actual* results of your *actual* data analysis under the null distribution.
Are they in the middle?
Are they in the tails?


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](22-more-inference_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-8-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


Are these results in harmony or conflict with the null?

## Null distribution versus reality {.smaller}

Locate the *actual* results of your *actual* data analysis under the null distribution.
Are they in the middle?
Are they in the tails?


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](22-more-inference_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-9-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


Are these results in harmony or conflict with the null?

## *p*-value {.smaller}

::: incremental
The $p$-value is the probability of being *even farther out* in the tails of the null distribution than your results already were.

-   if this number is very low, then your results would be out of the ordinary if the null were true, so maybe the null was never true to begin with;

-   if this number is high, then your results may be perfectly compatible with the null.
:::

## *p*-value {.smaller}

*p*-value is the fraction of the histogram area shaded red:


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](22-more-inference_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-10-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


. . .

Big ol' *p*-value.
Null looks plausible

## *p*-value {.smaller}

*p*-value is the fraction of the histogram area shaded red:


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](22-more-inference_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-11-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


. . .

*p*-value is basically zero.
Null looks bogus.

## *p*-value {.smaller}

*p*-value is the fraction of the histogram area shaded red:


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](22-more-inference_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-12-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


. . .

*p*-value is...kinda small?
Null looks...?

## Discernibility level {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   How do we decide if the *p*-value is big enough or small enough?

-   Pick a threshold $\alpha\in[0,\,1]$ called the *discernibility level*:

    -   If $p\text{-value} < \alpha$, reject null and accept alternative;
    -   If $p\text{-value} \geq \alpha$, fail to reject null;

-   Standard choices: $\alpha=0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15$.
:::

## Pop quiz! (not really)

We will do a few multiple choice questions to check-in about statistical inference:

## What is $b_1$? {.smaller}


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](22-more-inference_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-13-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


::: columns
::: column
a.  -1 / 2
b.  0
c.  1 / 4
:::

::: column
d.  1 / 2
e.  1
f.  2
:::
:::

## What is $b_1$? {.smaller}


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](22-more-inference_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-14-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


::: columns
::: column
a.  -1 / 2
b.  0
c.  1 / 4
:::

::: column
d.  1 / 2 (*correct*)
e.  1
f.  2
:::
:::

## What is $b_0$? {.smaller}


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](22-more-inference_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-15-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

::: column
a.  1
b.  1.75
:::

::: column
c.  2
d.  3
:::
:::

## What is $b_0$? {.smaller}


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](22-more-inference_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-16-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


::: columns
::: column
a.  1
b.  1.75
:::

::: column
c.  2
d.  3 (*correct*)
:::
:::

## Which of these could be a bootstrap sample? {.smaller}

This is the original dataset:


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Rows: 5
Columns: 1
$ scores <dbl> 1.563, -0.515, 1.206, -0.411, 0.523
```


:::
:::


| A      |     |     | B      |     |     | C      |     |     | D      |
|--------|-----|-----|--------|-----|-----|--------|-----|-----|--------|
| -0.515 |     |     | 1.563  |     |     | -0.515 |     |     | -0.522 |
| -0.411 |     |     | -0.411 |     |     | -0.411 |     |     | 1.12   |
| 1.563  |     |     | -0.515 |     |     | -0.411 |     |     | 1.206  |
| -0.515 |     |     |        |     |     | 1.563  |     |     | 0.68   |
| -0.515 |     |     |        |     |     | 1.563  |     |     | 0.83   |
|        |     |     |        |     |     | 0.523  |     |     |        |
|        |     |     |        |     |     | 1.563  |     |     |        |

## Which of these could be a bootstrap sample? {.smaller}

This is the original dataset:


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Rows: 5
Columns: 1
$ scores <dbl> 1.563, -0.515, 1.206, -0.411, 0.523
```


:::
:::


| A (*correct*) |     |     | B      |     |     | C      |     |     | D      |
|---------------|-----|-----|--------|-----|-----|--------|-----|-----|--------|
| -0.515        |     |     | 1.563  |     |     | -0.515 |     |     | -0.522 |
| -0.411        |     |     | -0.411 |     |     | -0.411 |     |     | 1.12   |
| 1.563         |     |     | -0.515 |     |     | -0.411 |     |     | 1.206  |
| -0.515        |     |     |        |     |     | 1.563  |     |     | 0.68   |
| -0.515        |     |     |        |     |     | 1.563  |     |     | 0.83   |
|               |     |     |        |     |     | 0.523  |     |     |        |
|               |     |     |        |     |     | 1.563  |     |     |        |

## Which could be the null distribution of the test? {.smaller}

For these hypotheses

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_0&: \beta_1=5\\
H_A&: \beta_1\neq 5.
\end{aligned}
$$


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](22-more-inference_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-21-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Which could be the null distribution of the test? {.smaller}

For these hypotheses

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_0&: \beta_1=5\\
H_A&: \beta_1\neq 5.
\end{aligned}
$$


::: {.cell}
